perturbation.original_text: Client will pay Excite its variable revenue share payments within thirty (30) days after the close of each month.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will pay Excite its variable revenue share payments within ninety (90) days after the close of each month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline from 30 to 90 days could create cash flow issues for Excite, conflicting with standard commercial practice and potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under New York Law
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Extending the payment period to 90 days without a valid business reason could be interpreted as a breach of this duty, especially if it causes financial hardship to Excite.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 1-304 Obligation of Good Faith Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 1, PART 3 Section 1--304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment period from 30 to 90 days, potentially causing financial hardship, which could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC 1-304, states that "Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." Extending the payment deadline significantly without a valid reason, as suggested by the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, could be interpreted as a breach of this obligation, making the contract unenforceable under this law.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement if the other party materially breaches its obligations hereunder and such breach remains uncured for thirty (30) days following the notice to the breaching party of the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement if the other party materially breaches its obligations hereunder and such breach remains uncured for ten (10) days following the notice to the breaching party of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from 30 to 10 days could be considered unreasonably short under New York law, especially for complex commercial agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Reasonable Notice and Opportunity to Cure under New York Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Filmline Productions, Inc. v. United Artists Corp., 662 N.Y.S.2d 9, 11 (1st Dep't 1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts generally require a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach before termination. A 10-day cure period may be deemed insufficient, particularly considering the complexity of the agreement and the potential for significant damages resulting from termination, thus contradicting the principle of reasonableness.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that such a reduction could be deemed insufficient and contradict the principle of reasonableness under New York law. Since New York courts generally require a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach before termination, the change introduces a time-sensitive obligation that is inconsistent with legal requirements. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction,' where a deadline violates state law, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Client will not use User Data to directly or indirectly solicit any Excite users either individually or in the aggregate during the term of this Agreement and for a period of twelve (12) months following the expiration or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will not use User Data to directly or indirectly solicit any Excite users either individually or in the aggregate during the term of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years following the expiration or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the restriction on using user data for solicitation to five years may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, particularly if it unduly restricts Client's ability to compete.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions, a five-year restriction on soliciting customers based on user data obtained during the agreement could be seen as an unreasonable restraint, especially if the user data isn't considered a trade secret, thus violating the code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the restriction on the Client's ability to solicit Excite users from twelve months to five years after the termination of the agreement. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The law snippet explicitly states this general prohibition. The change substantially increases the restriction period, potentially creating an unreasonable restraint of trade, thus violating the code.
